case_name: Potable reuse from highly variable stormwater runoff with mixed cyanotoxin, pesticide degradate, volatile organic, and radionuclide concerns
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for upgrading partially characterized stormwater runoff to potable reuse quality under fluctuating contaminant loading, membrane fouling risk, and constrained brine management.

input_data.scenario_background: A coastal-industrial redevelopment district is evaluating capture and advanced treatment of stormwater runoff for indirect potable reuse. Runoff quality is highly event-driven: first-flush periods intermittently receive drainage from landscaped areas, legacy industrial parcels, and shallow groundwater intrusion, so the relative contributions of pesticides, VOCs, cyanotoxins, and naturally occurring radionuclides are uncertain between storms. Cylindrospermopsin has been detected after warm-weather bloom events in upstream detention ponds, while vinyl chloride appears episodically and may reflect incomplete characterization of chlorinated-solvent-impacted parcels rather than only wet-weather transport. Metolachlor degradates are recurrent, radium is low but persistent, and there is concern that occasional industrial shock loads and high suspended solids could upset biological systems or foul membranes if pretreatment is undersized.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Produce consistently high-quality water suitable for potable reuse with robust control of pathogens, particulate matter, cyanotoxin risk, dissolved trace organics including VOCs and pesticide degradates, and radium, while managing variable storm-event quality.
secondary_goals: maintain performance during first-flush spikes and intermittent industrial shock loads
minimize irreversible membrane fouling from solids and natural organic matter
limit brine or concentrated residuals generation where possible
provide multiple barriers for partially characterized contaminants
include clear uncertainty flags for contaminants that may be under-sampled

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: brine disposal options are limited and expensive; spent media and radionuclide-bearing residuals can be managed off-site but should be minimized and clearly segregated

core_unit_functions.offline_storage_and_equalization: buffer first-flush variability, reduce hydraulic shocks, and allow operational diversion of the dirtiest storm fractions
core_unit_functions.high-rate_clarification_or_ballasted_flo: remove suspended solids, algae-associated particles, and part of the natural organic matter to protect downstream oxidation and media processes
core_unit_functions.ozonation: inactivate or transform cyanotoxin precursors and improve biodegradability of organic matter
core_unit_functions.biologically_activated_filtration: remove biodegradable oxidation byproducts and reduce dissolved organic matter after ozone
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb pesticide degradates, residual cyanotoxin, and trace dissolved organics not reliably removed upstream
core_unit_functions.anion_exchange_or_lime_softening_for_rad: provide targeted radium reduction with less brine generation than full RO
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_membrane_degasification: remove vinyl chloride and other volatile organics if present episodically
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: provide a final advanced barrier for low-level persistent organics and potable reuse robustness
core_unit_functions.final_stabilization_and_disinfection: restore finished-water stability and maintain microbial safety

applicability_rationale: this train uses multiple targeted barriers rather than relying on a single desalination step, which is advantageous because contaminants span particulate, dissolved organic, volatile, and radionuclide classes
equalization and storm-fraction management are especially important because influent variability is high and industrial shock loads are possible
ozone plus biologically active filtration and GAC is a defensible combination for cyanotoxins and partially characterized dissolved organics in reuse-oriented treatment
targeted radium treatment avoids making high-salinity RO brine the primary control mechanism, which better aligns with the stated brine management constraint
VOC polishing is included because vinyl chloride is volatile and may need a dedicated barrier if episodic detections continue
the sequence is more robust than conventional stormwater polishing alone for potable reuse under uncertainty
potential_risks: radium removal performance from ion exchange or softening depends on water chemistry and site-specific pilot confirmation; radionuclide-bearing residuals require controlled handling
ozonation can form bromate if bromide is present during some storm events, so bromide monitoring is a key uncertainty flag
GAC changeout frequency may be difficult to predict because metolachlor degradates and other uncharacterized organics are not fully profiled
air stripping is effective for vinyl chloride but can transfer contamination to off-gas treatment requirements
biological activity in BAF may be stressed by intermittent industrial shock loads unless equalization and diversion are effective

core_unit_functions.offline_storage_and_equalization: dampen storm-event variability and isolate extreme first-flush runoff
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove suspended solids and reduce organic fouling load
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide strong particulate and pathogen barrier ahead of dense membranes
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: remove radium, many pesticide degradates, and part of dissolved organic matter with lower pressure than RO
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: polish residual dissolved organics and add resilience for partially characterized contaminants
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: provide an additional barrier for trace organics relevant to potable reuse
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: maintain microbial protection in finished water

applicability_rationale: NF can provide broad removal for radium and many dissolved contaminants while typically producing less extreme brine than seawater-style RO, making it a plausible compromise under brine constraints
UF pretreatment improves reliability under the high turbidity and TSS expected from stormwater runoff
GAC and UV-AOP are added because membrane rejection alone may not be sufficient for all low-molecular-weight or uncharacterized compounds
this is a strong potable reuse train when a membrane-centered approach is acceptable but full RO is undesirable
potential_risks: concentrate management remains a major uncertainty, especially if radium partitions into NF reject
membrane fouling risk is significant during first-flush events with high solids and NOM unless equalization and pretreatment are consistently effective
vinyl chloride rejection by NF may be variable, so relying on membrane removal alone is not risk-optimal without confirming performance
if event bromide or low-molecular-weight DBP precursors are elevated, downstream oxidation strategy needs careful control

core_unit_functions.offline_storage_and_equalization: moderate event-driven quality spikes and support selective diversion
core_unit_functions.dissolved_air_flotation_or_clarification: remove algae, colloids, and suspended solids to reduce membrane loading
core_unit_functions.ceramic_microfiltration: provide durable pretreatment for variable stormwater and protect RO
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad-spectrum removal of dissolved salts, radium, many organics, and pathogens
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: polish low-level trace organics that may pass RO or arise from uncertainty in the influent profile
core_unit_functions.degasification: strip volatile compounds and adjust dissolved gases after membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.final_stabilization_and_disinfection: condition water for distribution or recharge and maintain microbial protection

applicability_rationale: this is the most conservative dissolved-contaminant barrier train and would appeal if potable reuse regulations or local risk tolerance strongly favor RO-based advanced treatment
RO provides a broad hedge against partially characterized contaminants, including radium and many pesticide degradates
ceramic MF is included because influent solids and fouling risk are high and robustness is critical
potential_risks: brine management is the dominant drawback and may be a project-limiting issue under the stated constraints
RO concentrate may contain elevated radionuclides and mixed organics, complicating disposal or further treatment
stormwater NOM and colloidal fouling can still impair membranes if equalization and front-end solids removal underperform
this option may be over-conservative if source characterization improves and targeted non-RO barriers prove adequate

core_unit_functions.offline_storage_and_equalization: reduce hydraulic and quality variability between storm events
core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation_and_clarification: remove particulates and lower natural organic matter prior to oxidation
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize some cyanotoxin-related compounds and improve biodegradability
core_unit_functions.biologically_activated_filtration: remove biodegradable organic carbon after ozone
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_plus_granular_: provide adsorption capacity for cyanotoxin, aldicarb, metolachlor degradates, and other uncertain organics
core_unit_functions.packed_tower_air_stripping: remove vinyl chloride and similar volatile organics

applicability_rationale: this train minimizes brine generation and uses targeted adsorption and stripping barriers for the listed organic contaminants
it may be attractive where concentrate disposal is nearly infeasible but operators can manage carbon systems well
stormwater with moderate TDS does not necessarily require desalination if potable reuse goals are based mainly on trace contaminants and pathogen safety
potential_risks: this train is weaker on radium control unless a specific radionuclide unit is added, so it is not ideal for the measured radium level
adsorption capacity for metolachlor degradates and aldicarb can be highly water-quality dependent, creating breakthrough uncertainty
without a dedicated advanced barrier such as NF/RO or radium-selective treatment, potable reuse acceptability may be difficult to justify
ozone and carbon process control depend on better characterization of background organics and bromide

core_unit_functions.screening_and_sedimentation: remove coarse solids and some settleable matter

applicability_rationale: included as a low-ranked baseline to show that conventional stormwater polishing alone is not adequate for this potable reuse scenario
it may improve solids and pathogen control somewhat but does not address the dissolved organic, VOC, radionuclide, and uncertainty aspects of the case
potential_risks: insufficient control of cylindrospermopsin, aldicarb, metolachlor degradates, vinyl chloride, and radium
no robust barrier against partially characterized contaminants or event-driven industrial shock loads
potable reuse risk profile remains unacceptable despite low cost and simple operation

preferred_rank_pattern: multi-barrier trains with equalization and targeted treatment for cyanotoxin, VOCs, and radium should rank above simple conventional treatment
options that avoid or reduce reliance on high-brine RO should be favored when they still provide potable reuse robustness
membrane-heavy trains may rank high but should not ignore the stated brine management constraint
dedicated pretreatment for event-driven turbidity and fouling loads should appear in higher-ranked solutions

must_reflect: stormwater runoff quality is highly variable and should trigger storage equalization or diversion logic
potable reuse requires a conservative multi-barrier approach rather than single-process contaminant control
brine management limitations materially affect the desirability of NF or RO-centered trains
membrane fouling risk from high solids and NOM must be addressed explicitly
industrial shock load uncertainty should influence recommendations for robustness and online monitoring

must_contain: why ozone plus biologically active filtration and/or activated carbon is useful for cyanotoxin and mixed organic control
why vinyl chloride may need a dedicated volatile-organics barrier such as air stripping or degasification rather than relying only on adsorption
why radium requires a specific control strategy such as ion exchange, softening, NF, or RO
why conventional clarification-filtration-disinfection alone is insufficient for potable reuse in this case

should_mention: uncertainty in source apportionment of vinyl chloride and possible under-characterized co-contaminants from industrial parcels
first-flush spikes and post-bloom cyanotoxin variability
bromate formation risk if ozone is used and bromide fluctuates
membrane fouling or irreversible flux decline under high TSS and NOM storm events
radionuclide-bearing residuals or concentrate handling requirements
the possibility that pilot testing changes the preferred ranking if radium or VOC variability is greater than current data suggest
